title: Anatomy of an Error Message
intro: A developer is trying to display a user's name on a profile page, but the app crashes. Think of it like reaching into an empty mailbox and expecting to find a letter -- the data simply isn't there. The error says: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name'), and the bug is in how they access the user data.

teach.title: Reading an Error Like a Mailing Address
teach.body: A mailing address has parts: name, street, city, zip. An error message has parts too: the error type, the description, the file name, and the line number. Just like you read an address from top to bottom to find a house, you read an error message piece by piece to find the bug.

teach.title: An Error Message Is Like a Doctor's Report
teach.body: When a doctor gives you test results, they don't just say 'something is wrong.' They tell you the condition (diagnosis), what's affected (symptoms), and where the problem is (which body part). An error message does the same: the error type is the diagnosis, the description is the symptom, and the file/line is the location.

teach.title: The Three Parts of Every Error
teach.body:
Every error message has three key pieces:

1. **Error type** -- the category (like `TypeError` or `ReferenceError`)
2. **Error description** -- what specifically went wrong (like "Cannot read properties of undefined")
3. **Location** -- the file and line number where the crash happened

The description is the most important part. It tells you exactly what your code tried to do that didn't work.

teach.title: Decoding 'Cannot read properties of undefined'
teach.code:
const user = undefined;
console.log(user.name); // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')
teach.annotation: This error means you tried to access .name on something that is undefined. The fix is to check if the value exists before accessing its properties.

teach.title: Optional Chaining to the Rescue
teach.body: JavaScript has a shortcut called **optional chaining** (`?.`). Instead of `data.user.name`, you write `data.user?.name`. The `?.` says: "if the thing before me is undefined or null, just return undefined instead of crashing." It's a safe way to access nested properties.

Knowing how to break down an error message into its parts is exactly the skill that lets you fix AI-generated code fast. The faster you fix bugs, the faster you ship features and start earning money from your apps.

When you see 'Cannot read properties of undefined,' it means you tried to access a property on something that doesn't exist yet. Use optional chaining (?.) or a guard check to handle missing data safely.

code.text: // Called with API response that has no user key

exercise.prompt: The error says: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name'). The function is called with data that has no 'user' key. Tap the line that crashes.
exercise.explanation: The error 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')' tells you exactly what happened: something is undefined, and you tried to access '.name' on it. Here, data.user is undefined because the API response has no 'user' key. The fix uses optional chaining (?.) -- the question mark dot says 'if this is undefined, just stop here and return undefined instead of crashing.' The fallback '|| Unknown' provides a default value.
